[2020-07-24 03:56] cameron_real (Real): But anyways synopsis for my game:

My game is called BOS, or Battle Of Skill

You are a ruler of a nation going to war against another nation. You have recruited three wizards to help you in your battle. These wizards are pretty good at magic. They can use an ability specific to them, and they can call upon monsters called Summons. But you, as a ruler, are far better at magic than your wizards. You can cast ten different spells at the cost of vim, the magical energy source of this world, and can summon the most powerful form of monster, a Boss.

Depending on the color of your soul, you can use certain cards. You have a choice between red, yellow, and blue. Red is fiery, scaling, aggression. The more aggressive you are, even in situations where it might be bad for you, the better. Red is risk/reward.

Yellow is natural strength. It has the strongest summons and can heal you. It can be aggro or defensive depending on how you build your deck.

Blue is darker, and watery-er. It has the most powerful spells in the game, but it requires that you make it to the late-game where you have enough vim to play them. It requires you to play perfect and is the most skill-intensive deck of them all.

[2020-07-24 04:02] cameron_real (Real): The idea game-development-wise is that there's enough variety in the game to not have the meta solved, and with the addition of the reserves pile in competitive play, if a "best deck" is found, then it can be countered by any other deck by changing the reserves you get. At which point the best deck needs to adapt back, etc, etc
[2020-07-24 04:04] elyot: I mean that's not the problem, the problem is that when your meta converges to some mixed nash equilibrium then laddering just turns into playing optimally against the finite support of whatever that nash distribution is, and if you're just playing the same openings etc. over and over again then there's no source of variety
[2020-07-24 04:04] elyot: it's even worse if you yourself are just playing the same deck over and over again
[2020-07-24 04:07] cameron_real (Real): Ok that makes sense, but I can't imagine the game having a default opening for each deck. Maybe thats just wishful thinking but the open beta will also give me insight to that as well
[2020-07-24 04:07] cameron_real (Real): Just for the record though, this is going to be a physical tcg
[2020-07-24 04:07] elyot: People solve games ridiculously quickly
[2020-07-24 04:07] elyot: Though yeah it's much more slowly if it's a physical game
[2020-07-24 04:08] cameron_real (Real): That's what I'm hoping lol
[2020-07-24 04:08] cameron_real (Real): I want people to try to crack this game so I can see what needs to be improved and why
[2020-07-24 04:08] cameron_real (Real): The why part is the most important ofc
[2020-07-24 04:10] cameron_real (Real): I'm hoping that I can create general support cards go against many decks at the same time, but don't give so much advantage that it becomes impossible to win
[2020-07-24 04:11] cameron_real (Real): I also really want to be able to step down into the open beta once the "best deck" has been found in order to come in and say "what? this deck sucks. here's how you beat this." and then the meta continues. Might be wishful thinking but again, we'll have to see.

[2020-07-24 06:58] elyot: @Deleted User The best way to beat any addiction IMO is to have something else meaningful to you that you can be passionate about, if somebody is gaming too much to the point that it's interfering with the rest of life then probably there's other questions about the rest of your life that are more relevant than the gaming itself
[2020-07-24 07:00] elyot: Like what kind of person you are, where do you want to be, what do you love, what do you need to expose yourself to, etc.
[2020-07-24 07:02] elyot: Figure out those things, spend some time doing travel or being with family/friends or live in the woods for a week or something, reset yourself a bit and it will be easier to move forward spending time the way you want
[2020-07-24 07:02] elyot: Also cognitive behavioural therapy and actually making plans/goals, writing them down, having other ppl hold you accountable, etc.

[2020-07-31 02:20] cameron_real (Real): Hey devs, how often do you interact with your dedicated fanbase directly? As a developer myself, how should I be treating the community I have?
[2020-07-31 02:21] elyot: I think it depends what your goals are, like if you're actively seeking a lot of feedback or getting a lot of questions/complaints or responding to a crisis or an opportunity
[2020-07-31 02:22] elyot: If your game is really small then talking to individual people in your community can be a high-value way to drive a lot of engagement, if you have a million players then you need more public forms of communication and also more private ones with people who you can trust to bring you high-value feedback
[2020-07-31 02:23] elyot: Reddit was very important for us early on, and I would do regular blog/youtube updates
[2020-07-31 02:23] elyot: I think it really brought a lot of benefit in keeping the community engaged, getting feedback, learning of opportunities and problems, etc.

[2020-07-31 05:10] elyot: I ended up getting an opening named after me (the Elyot Animus) but I would debate strats all the time with the community and stream my games
[2020-07-31 05:10] elyot: I think if you're making a competitive strategy game then it's pretty important that folks on your team are top players and are participating in that discussion
[2020-07-31 05:12] elyot: Our game doesn't have a "meta" so I can't really speak to that part of the question, but I think that when the community has misconceptions about the balance of the game, it can be important to educate them (and also to understand where the misconception comes from, because sometimes things that feel "too strong" are actually just "not fun")
[2020-07-31 05:12] .bky_1556 (bky): Prismata doesn't have a meta?
[2020-07-31 05:13] elyot: It doesn't have pre-game deck selection
[2020-07-31 05:13] elyot: And it doesn't have hidden information so there's no underlying distribution that you need to consider when making a play, people just play optimally, it's the same as chess
[2020-07-31 05:14] elyot: Like maybe you can study a certain opening more if you think that an opponent is more likely to select from that type of line, but otherwise there's not like, a mixed nash equilibrium part of the game the way there is in other games where there's deck or character selection before the game starts

[2020-09-09 19:42] elyot: Yes but it would be a lot of work to redo the entire input for a controller, would be less good than a mouse, and the audience for hardcore strategy games is almost entirely on PC
[2020-09-09 19:45] Deleted User (Deleted User): is it because of the PC master race
[2020-09-09 19:46] elyot: It's because strategy games often require precise clicking, which favours a mouse, and they target older, more technical audiences instead of children, so they're more likely to own PCs and use them as their primary gaming hardware instead of consoles
[2020-09-09 19:48] elyot: Like, StarCraft had an N64 port. I don't think they necessarily lost money on it, but it was a far inferior experience and they would not release such a thing today. And for a smaller game it makes even less sense
[2020-09-09 19:53] Deleted User (Deleted User): they do? I imagine pris or runeterra does not req precise clicking
[2020-09-09 19:54] Deleted User (Deleted User): and don't consoles fix this problem via aimbotting
[2020-09-09 19:54] Deleted User (Deleted User): eh i guess with shooters there's only 1 thing to click
[2020-09-09 19:55] siepu: runeterra is max 6 units on board and like 10 cards
[2020-09-09 19:55] siepu: have you ever seen a prismata game?
[2020-09-09 19:55] siepu: then I respect him even more as he got good while being legally blind
[2020-09-09 19:56] Deleted User (Deleted User): like i imagine it can't be worse than say skyrim or other heavy inventory games
[2020-09-09 19:56] siepu: (MtG still has no good mobile port for exactly that reason)
[2020-09-09 19:56] li_fi_ (LiFi): skyrim doesn't have a chess clock running down when you open your backpack or w/e
[2020-09-09 19:56] elyot: the most popular RTS, 4x, tactics, etc. games tend to place high demands on clicking accuracy to the point where doing it with a joystick is impractical. Discrete games (xcom etc.) can be OK on consoles and Prismata kinda lies at that boundary where it would still be annoying to click exactly n dudes in a stack but it otherwise could have a lot of new shortcuts added to make it mostly playable
[2020-09-09 19:57] siepu: the issue is prismata like mtg unlike xcom has potentially infinite boards
[2020-09-09 19:57] elyot: the "requires precise clicking" is more of an argument for why strategy games have cultivated a large audience on PC rather than console, rather than an argument for why individual strategy games couldn't be good on console
[2020-09-09 19:57] Deleted User (Deleted User): lifespan for the infinites?
[2020-09-09 19:57] elyot: Like there is rayman + mario kingdom battle and it's a console exclusive
[2020-09-09 19:58] Deleted User (Deleted User): ah yes gotcha eloyot
[2020-09-09 19:58] Deleted User (Deleted User): i'm surprised children with consoles don't like strategy games
[2020-09-09 19:58] elyot: yes exactly, grid-based titles are much better for console in general
